title: Breaking Par at Grandezza a Success!
content: Breaking Par at Grandezza a Success! Estero residents turned out in force to support our local schools at the 13th annual “Breaking Par for Education” held February 1 & 2 at the Club at Grandezza. The Gala and Golf Tournament support LEAD Estero, a non-profit created and run by Estero High School students for Estero High School students and their community. They provide scholarships and educational and leadership opportunities for the future movers and shakers here in our community. The “Breaking Par” Gala is known for its enthusiastic themes and this year was no different, with an intriguing “Murder Mystery” theme that engaged everyone who attended. Guest of Honor this year was Steve Sarkozy, the Village of Estero Manager, who has championed enhancements and collaboration with the school district. Together the events succeeded in grossing over $100,000 to provide educational opportunities to students right here in Estero, and we THANK EVERYONE who took part! Engage Estero is proud to be a partner and sponsor of the event and encourages anyone who couldn't make it to the event, but still wants to support this fine effort, to visit https://leadestero.com . title: Forgetfulness: Aging or Dementia?
content: Forgetfulness: Aging or Dementia? Determining Whether Forgetfulness is simply a matter of Aging or Whether it is the Onset of Dementia Allan Bowditch Chief Communications Officer Introduction. While some forgetfulness is normal during aging, these concerns can trigger anxiety, especially if the changes in memory are more frequent or impactful. Recognizing that some memory lapses may be part of normal aging is important. Still, when forgetfulness starts to interfere with daily life, it’s always a good idea to consult a healthcare professional to rule out any underlying medical conditions. For many, aging itself can be a source of anxiety. The changes that come with growing older, including changes in memory and cognitive function, can be distressing. People may worry about losing their youthful vitality and sharpness, and forgetfulness can be an early, visible sign of this process. People fear it could be the first sign of broader cognitive decline, affecting their ability to think, solve problems, or make decisions. One of the primary fears people have is that memory loss could be a sign of dementia or Alzheimer's disease 1 . These conditions are associated with a decline in cognitive functions, and people are understandably worried that they or their loved ones may be experiencing the early stages of a serious neurological condition. The Difference Between Forgetfulness and Dementia. Determining the difference between forgetfulness associated with regular aging and forgetfulness related to dementia can be challenging, but several key factors can help distinguish between the two. Severity of Memory Loss: Aging: As people age, they might occasionally forget names, appointments, or where they left things, but they can usually remember them later or after some prompting. The memory loss tends to be mild and does not interfere significantly with daily life. Dementia: In dementia, memory loss becomes more pronounced and consistent. Individuals may forget important information, such as the names of close family members or where they live. Memory loss tends to worsen over time and can significantly affect their ability to function independently. Frequency and Impact: Aging: Forgetfulness in older adults is typically infrequent and isolated. They can still perform daily tasks independently, and their memory loss does not lead to major disruptions in their daily routines. Dementia: Forgetfulness due to dementia tends to be more frequent and impacts daily functioning. It might affect an individual’s ability to perform everyday tasks like managing finances, following a conversation, or remembering how to use familiar objects (e.g., a phone or stove). Ability to Recall with Cues: Aging: When older adults forget something, they often can recall the information with some prompting or by retracing their steps. For example, they may eventually remember where they placed their keys. Dementia: People with dementia may have difficulty recalling information even with cues or reminders. For example, they may repeatedly ask the same questions or forget what they were just told, even after being reminded. Changes in Other Cognitive Functions: Aging: Aging-related forgetfulness typically doesn’t affect other cognitive functions like judgment, reasoning, and problem-solving. Dementia: Dementia affects multiple areas of cognition. In addition to memory loss, people may experience confusion, difficulty with language (e.g., forgetting or misusing words), trouble with spatial awareness, impaired judgment, and changes in personality or behavior. Onset and Progression: Aging: Forgetfulness related to aging tends to develop gradually over time and remains relatively stable. It doesn’t usually progress rapidly. Dementia: Dementia typically has a progressive onset. Symptoms worsen over time, and the decline in memory and other cognitive abilities becomes more severe. Personality and Behavior Changes: Aging: There are usually no significant changes in personality or behavior with typical aging, aside from occasional frustration or anxiety related to memory lapses. Dementia: People with dementia may experience noticeable changes in personality or behavior, such as becoming unusually irritable, withdrawn, or socially inappropriate. They may also have mood swings or display aggression or paranoia. Awareness of Memory Issues: Aging: Older adults are usually aware of their memory lapses and may express frustration or concern about them. Dementia: In the early stages of dementia, some individuals may be aware of their cognitive decline, but as the disease progresses, they often lose awareness of their memory problems. In later stages, they may not realize they have difficulty remembering or may deny it. If there is significant concern about memory issues or cognitive decline, it's essential to seek professional evaluation from a healthcare provider. They can conduct thorough assessments, including cognitive tests and medical imaging, to determine whether the forgetfulness is part of normal aging or an early sign of dementia. Reference Understanding and Coping with Dementia (The difference between Dementia and Alzheimer’s Disease) Allan Bowditch. 5.09.2024. https://esterotoday.com/dementia/ The above information is provided by Engage Estero as guidance and is not medical advice or diagnosis. Always consult with a physician if you are concerned about a medical cause for forgetfulness. Be Informed, Get Engaged, and Make an Impact! title: The Status of Our Local Economy
category: City News; Economic Development; Growth; Recent News
content: The Status of Our Local Economy The 'Southwest Florida’s Economic and Real Estate Outlook—Q4 2024 and 2025' report analyzes economic and real estate trends specifically for Estero, Florida. Compiled by Florida Gulf Coast University’s Regional Economic Research Institute, it emphasizes the region's economic diversification, real estate performance, and key indicators. Key insights include: The Cape Coral-Fort Myers MSA improved its Industrial Diversification Index (IDI) to 5.089, ranking 11th among Florida's 22 MSAs. This was driven by growth in light industrial development. The Naples-Marco Island MSA remains strong, ranking 2nd in the state despite a slight decline in its IDI, with Leisure and Hospitality employment showing significant recovery. The Punta Gorda MSA shows less economic diversification and relies heavily on industries serving older populations. Overall, the Southwest Florida region's IDI increased to 5.202, reflecting progress toward a more stable economy. Zachary Katkin Communications The report also highlights the ongoing challenges of developing a stable middle class in the region, where employment opportunities are largely concentrated in a few key sectors: Government Services, Healthcare Services, and Grocery and Food Services. The reliance on tourism, healthcare, and retail has led to a job market primarily supporting the wealthy, with limited opportunities for widespread economic growth. title: Engage Estero Celebrates 25 Years of Impact
content: Engage Estero Celebrates 25 Years of Impact On January 15, 2025 volunteers and civic leaders currently and formerly active in impacting the future of the Greater Estero area gathered to celebrate 25 years of grassroots involvement resulting in a distinctive and established community which actively participates in the growth and direction of its future. Speakers Bob Lienesch, Jim Gilmartin and Brawley Adams spoke on the history, present and future of both Engage Estero and Estero as a community. We celebrated the many individuals who contributed to the formation and appearance of the area. One hour was not enough to cover it all! For more about the Goals, Achievements, Leadership, Founders, History and Memories of Engage Estero, use the links below to visit our page dedicated to celebrating all the organization has been and will continue to be. Our Goals | Our Achievements | Our Leadership | Our Founders | Our Memories | Our History Background For 25 years, Engage Estero has stood as a beacon of original research and communication, tirelessly dedicated to our community's well-being. We are a nonpartisan, nonpolitical, IRS-designated 501c3 nonprofit dedicated to empowering the community through evidence-based, unbiased information on key issues. Originally established in 1999 as a grassroots organization of residential community leaders known as ECCO, we became the Estero Council of Community Leaders (ECCL) nonprofit in 2004. In 2023, the organization transitioned to its current name, Engage Estero . By researching local and regional issues and providing impartial insights, we enable citizens to make informed decisions that shape a thriving future. Through forums, reports, educational outreach, surveys, and partnerships with other nonprofits, we focus on, but are not limited to, critical topics like traffic, community resilience, development, and water quality. title: The Village of Estero Commemorates Don Eslick: A Legacy of Vision and Community!
content: The Village of Estero Commemorates Don Eslick: A Legacy of Vision and Community! On January 22, 2025, the Village of Estero honored the incredible contributions of Don Eslick—affectionately known as “Mr. Estero” and shared a commemoration plaque that symbolizes not just a person but a vision that we should continue to embrace. One of Don’s crowning achievements was his key role in incorporating Estero in 2014. This was more than a milestone; it was a battle to protect our identity! With Don’s leadership, the Estero Council of Community Leaders (ECCL), now Engage Estero, prevented Bonita Springs' attempt to annex our cherished town and empowered us with home rule. Our community now thrives with enhanced building standards and sustainable growth policies that ensure Estero remains the beautiful sanctuary we cherish. Jim Gilmartin President Don was a steadfast protector of our community’s character, ensuring Estero remained a vibrant and livable environment. Past Lee County Commissioner Frank Mann poignantly noted, "If the ECCL had not been there, we might have seen a large part of Lee County looking like wall-to-wall concrete." Don Eslick was not just a founding member of the ECCL—he was the driving force that fueled our community’s heart. From 2003 to 2018, his passion as President and Chairman reshaped our village, always with a laser focus on the residents' needs and desires. Estero is the vibrant enclave it is today, thanks in no small part to Don’s extraordinary foresight during its pivotal development phase post-2000! Don’s unwavering spirit and dedication have left an indelible mark on everyone who calls Estero home. He has given us more than service; he has given us a community rooted in values he championed daily. His work and his legacy continue to influence the area. The community remains committed to the vision he advocated for, ensuring Estero's prosperity for future generations. Finally, Don didn’t just serve; he built a community grounded in values we all cherish. As we celebrate his legacy, let’s recommit ourselves to this shared vision, ensuring that Estero remains a thriving hub for future generations. Let’s carry forward the legacy he so passionately created. Be Informed, Get Engaged, and Make an Impact! At Engage Estero, we believe in the strength of the community. content: The Engage Estero management, board members, and the volunteers who keep Estero looking beautiful wish you all a Happy and Enjoyable 2025. title: Discover Estero’s Exciting Transformation: The Village Sports Park is Coming to Elevate Your Lifestyle!
category: City News; Development / Community Planning; Recent News
content: Discover Estero’s Exciting Transformation: The Village Sports Park is Coming to Elevate Your Lifestyle! Village-Hub-Master-Plan by: Terry Flanagan Business Administrator Let’s face it—if you live in Estero, life centers around the sun, water, and that irresistible resort vibe that draws us in. Whether you’re a full-time resident or one of our beloved snowbirds, slipping into our comforting golf, tennis, and poolside conversation routines is too easy. But hold onto your hats because something extraordinary in our community is on the horizon, promising to elevate our lifestyle and quality of life to an entirely new level: the Village Sports Park. The Village has broken ground on the initial site work phase for the new Estero entertainment and sports venue. This phase includes 20 acres of site development and construction by High 5 Entertainment, which will anchor the new entertainment campus. It is scheduled to be completed in November 2025, with High 5 Entertainment opening for business. Over the next five (5) years, the Village will make significant investments in the Sports Park Campus. Located at Williams Road and Via Coconut Rd., this transformative addition will redefine what it means to live, play, and connect in Estero. Imagine a vibrant hub where fitness, fun, and community intertwine—a place designed to cater to all ages and interests. The Village Sports Park will open up exciting opportunities, from state-of-the-art sports facilities to social events that bring us all together. Estero Is Becoming a Destination Estero has been known for years as a quiet, relaxed place to live. But with the new Village Sports Park, we’re evolving into something more—a destination. Think about it: Naples has long had the spotlight, but now we’re stepping up with a sports and recreation hub offering more diversity and appeal than you’ll find in Fort Myers or Bonita Springs. This isn’t about adding some soccer fields or a few pickleball courts (although we’ve got those, too). It’s about transforming Estero into a place that blends active living with the community spirit we all love. From football and lacrosse to basketball, wall climbing, and even controlled racing, there’s something for everyone here—no matter your age or interest. More Than Sports: A Place to Connect What makes this park special is how it goes beyond sports. Sure, you can play pickleball or watch a soccer game, but you can also enjoy a pavilion Tai Chi class, join a book club, or meet friends for a lively political discussion. There’s space for radio-controlled sailboat races and landscape artists to set up their easels. And let’s not forget the social aspect. The park creates a gathering spot for everyone, whether meeting up with your pickleball group, prepping for a game with your team, or just enjoying the buzz of activity around you. It’s a way to connect with neighbors and friends beyond the gates of your HOA. Solving Problems, Adding Value One of the most innovative things about this park is that it's not just about sports. It's about solving problems we’ve all noticed. For instance, the park provides a perfect solution to complaints about pickleball noise (perhaps you’ve made a few yourself). Creating a dedicated space for pickleball enthusiasts keeps the sport fun and social while preserving peace in your neighborhood. It's about addressing issues and adding value to our community. It also offers amenities that individual communities just can’t. Most HOAs aren’t adding lacrosse fields or rock-climbing walls anytime soon. But now, thanks to the Village Sports Park, those options are right in your backyard. A Park for All Ages The park’s uniqueness lies in bringing people together across generations. It’s not just a sports park; it’s a community park designed to suit the lifestyle of residents, whether you’re 6 or 65 (or closer to 85!). It's a place where everyone will be welcomed and valued, where kids play soccer, parents cheer from the sidelines, and grandparents enjoy a Tai Chi session. Estero’s Bright New Future The Village Sports Park is a vibrant hub that will transform Estero into a dynamic community where everyone is included. Imagine a place where you can actively engage in sports, enjoy recreational activities or soak in the lively atmosphere as a spectator. The park is designed to enrich your life, foster connections among neighbors, and be a beacon for togetherness. So, when someone asks, “What’s new in Estero?” you can proudly tell them about this incredible new park changing the game for everyone. Whether you live here year-round or visit, this park will be yours. Prepare to embrace the opportunity to enhance your lifestyle and experience all the Village Sports Park offers! title: Joe and Joni Pavich Will be Honored at The Don Eslick Person of the Year Award Dinner on Feb. 27, 2025
content: Joe and Joni Pavich Will be Honored at The Don Eslick Person of the Year Award Dinner on Feb. 27, 2025 Join us on Thursday night, February 27, 2025, at the Estero Country Club at the Vines for a special occasion. We have the extraordinary opportunity to honor Joe and Joni Pavich, who will receive the prestigious Don Eslick Estero Person of the Year Award . This award recognizes their unwavering commitment and passionate belief in Estero for over 35 years. Joe and Joni have shown incredible dedication through their volunteer work with various organizations in Estero, such as the Chamber of Commerce, Rotary, Estero Forever Foundation, and Engage Estero. Their contributions are exemplary examples of community spirit and teamwork. The couple worked tirelessly in their new Real Estate business after they arrived in 1989. Joe’s efforts to attract potential new residents highlight his belief in Estero as a great place to live. He didn’t just promote the community; he built a sense of belonging and excitement that resonated with newcomers. Meanwhile, Joni’s meticulous organization and people and sales skills ensured every initiative ran smoothly and efficiently. It was a team effort. “Our mom gets credit for finding the Estero area. She was the one who pushed us to come here,” recounts their sons, Joe Jr. and Jason Pavich. Tickets Please join us on February 27th for an unforgettable evening! We’ll kick off the night with a delightful Happy Hour at 6 pm, where you can indulge in tables filled with assorted cheese, fresh berries, crisp vegetables, and crackers. Enjoy two complimentary tickets for wine or beer and for those looking for something special, a cash bar will be available for mixed drinks. At 6:50 p.m., we’ll transition to a sumptuous dinner featuring an exquisite selection of entrees to satisfy every palate. Choose from juicy Filet Mignon, flavorful Seared Black Cod, tender Chicken Breast, or a delicious Vegetarian option. Each meal is thoughtfully curated, including a fresh salad and a decadent dessert. So, mark your calendar and be part of a night to remember! Don't miss out on the highlight of the evening: the award presentation, which celebrates Joe and Joni’s exceptional achievements and contributions. Your presence at this event is a chance to show appreciation for their dedication to our community. Let’s acknowledge their hard work and inspire one another to continue building a brighter future for Estero. The Don Eslick Estero Person of the Year Award, sponsored by Engage Estero, is not just an annual recognition; it is a heartfelt tribute to a remarkable individual whose impact on our community is immeasurable. Don Eslick was not only a founding member of the Estero Council of Community Leaders - ECCL (now Engage Estero) but also the driving force behind the incorporation of the Village of Estero in 2014. His passion for enhancing the lives of those in our community set a standard for us all. We remember Don for what he accomplished, but we also strive to carry forward his incredible legacy!
